docs.text: Fourteenth Amendment Restoration ¶ Section 1 — National Citizenship and Supremacy ¶ The Fourteenth Amendment is reaffirmed as a guarantee of national citizenship and as a binding limitation on the States. No State shall invoke sovereignty, reserved powers, dignity, or immunity as a basis to evade or diminish the rights secured by that Amendment. Section 2 — Privileges or Immunities ¶ The privileges or immunities of citizens of the United States include all rights secured by this Constitution and by laws enacted pursuant thereto. No State shall abridge such privileges or immunities. The privileges or immunities secured by this Constitution, and by laws enacted pursuant thereto, shall apply to all persons and all territory subject to the jurisdiction or authority of the United States. No interpretation of this Constitution shall construe this clause narrowly or as without independent force. Section 3 — Due Process ¶ No State shall deprive any person of life, liberty, or property without due process of law. This guarantee secures substantive as well as procedural protections and applies to all rights secured by this Constitution. Section 4 — Equal Protection ¶ The Equal Protection of the laws guarantees equal civil status and equal protection against arbitrary, discriminatory, or unequal state action. No classification by a State shall be upheld unless consistent with this guarantee. Section 5 — Incorporation ¶ All rights enumerated in this Constitution are applicable to the States. No distinction shall be drawn between incorporated and unincorporated rights. Section 6 — Enforcement Power (Restored) ¶ Congress shall have power to enforce the provisions of this Amendment by appropriate legislation. Such power shall include the authority to define, prevent, remedy, and deter violations of this Amendment. This authority shall not be limited by judicial doctrines requiring congruence, proportionality, narrow tailoring, or deference to state sovereignty. Section 7 — Disqualification Clause Enforcement ¶ The disqualification prescribed by Section 3 of the Fourteenth Amendment is self-executing. No prior criminal conviction, impeachment, or congressional action shall be required for its enforcement. Congress may by law provide procedures for the determination and enforcement of such disqualification, but the absence of such procedures shall not bar its application by any court or election authority with jurisdiction, notwithstanding the absence of implementing legislation. Section 8 — Supersession ¶ Any prior judicial interpretation inconsistent with this Amendment or with the original purpose and enforcement authority of the Fourteenth Amendment is hereby superseded. Section 9 — Citizenship ¶ All persons born in the United States and subject to its laws are citizens of the United States and of the State wherein they reside. A person born in the United States shall be deemed subject to the jurisdiction of the United States at birth regardless of the citizenship, nationality, immigration status, or lawful presence of the person’s parents. The phrase “subject to the jurisdiction thereof” excludes only persons born: to foreign diplomatic officers accredited to the United States; to persons exercising sovereign authority on behalf of a foreign power; or to members of an invading or occupying military force. No other exception to birthright citizenship shall be recognized as a constitutional requirement. Congress may by law provide for the acquisition of citizenship on terms more permissive than those set forth in this Section.

docs.text: Judicial Access Amendment ¶ Section 1 ¶ The judicial power of the United States shall extend to all cases and controversies within the jurisdiction of the federal courts as established by this Constitution and by law. The requirement of a case or controversy shall be satisfied where adverse parties present a concrete dispute capable of judicial resolution. Section 2 ¶ No court of the United States shall decline to exercise jurisdiction otherwise properly conferred on the ground that a question presented is political, prudential, or inappropriate for judicial determination. Section 3 ¶ Congress may by law define and regulate the jurisdiction of the federal courts, including standards of standing, ripeness, and mootness. No judicially created doctrine shall limit such jurisdiction except as expressly provided in this Constitution. Section 4 ¶ Where jurisdiction is proper and facts sufficient to resolve a dispute are established, the courts of the United States shall decide the questions of law presented. Section 5 ¶ Any prior judicial interpretation inconsistent with this amendment is hereby superseded.

docs.text: Pardon Power Accountability Amendment ¶ Section 1 — Covered Officers ¶ The pardon power of the President shall not extend to any person who, at the time of the conduct for which pardon is sought: (a) held an office of the United States whose appointment required confirmation by the Senate; (b) served in an acting, interim, or temporary capacity in any such office; or (c) served in the Executive Office of the President or in any successor office designated by Congress, regardless of title or organizational form. Section 2 — Specificity Requirement ¶ Every pardon shall: (a) identify the individual by legal name; (b) specify the conduct pardoned; and (c) identify the federal statute or statues, including section numbers, to which the pardon applies. No pardon may use general, omnibus, or open-ended language such as “all offenses against the United States.” Section 3 — No Self-Amnesty and No Class Pardons ¶ No President may pardon himself or herself. No pardon may be issued to any class of persons defined by office, agency, function, or status. Section 4 — Effect of Violation ¶ Any pardon issued in violation of this Amendment shall have no legal force. Section 5 — Preservation of Clemency ¶ Nothing in this Amendment shall restrict the President’s power to grant pardons to persons not described in Section 1, except as limited by the requirements of Section 2. Section 6 — Effective Date ¶ This Amendment shall take effect upon ratification.

docs.text: 11th Amendment Repeal ¶ Section 1 ¶ The Eleventh Amendment to the Constitution of the United States is hereby repealed. Section 2 ¶ Neither the United States nor any State shall be immune, by reason of sovereignty, from suit in any court of the United States or of any court of any State arising under this Constitution, the laws of the United States, or treaties made under their authority. Section 3 ¶ Congress shall have power to regulate the remedies available in such suits, including limitations on damages, prospective relief, and procedural requirements. Section 4 ¶ No doctrine of sovereign immunity, whether derived from common law, constitutional interpretation, or judicial construction, shall bar jurisdiction otherwise granted by this Constitution or by law. Section 5 ¶ No immunity from suit or liability shall be recognized as a constitutional requirement unless expressly stated in this Constitution.

docs.text: Vesting Clause Amendment ¶ Section 1 ¶ The executive power of the United States is vested in the President and such officers, agencies, and instrumentalities as may be established by law. The vesting of executive power in the President shall not be construed to require that all persons exercising executive functions be subject to the President’s direction, supervision, or removal at will. Section 2 ¶ Congress may by law establish executive officers and agencies whose heads or members may be appointed for fixed terms and removed only for cause, or in such manner as Congress shall provide. The existence or exercise of executive authority by such officers or agencies shall not be deemed inconsistent with this Constitution. Section 3 ¶ No interpretation of this Constitution shall recognize an inherent or exclusive presidential authority to control, direct, or remove all executive officers solely by virtue of the Vesting Clause of Article II. Section 4 ¶ Any prior judicial interpretation inconsistent with this amendment is hereby superseded.

docs.text: Thoughts on the impending Rule 12(b) Motion to Dismiss ¶ 12(b)(6) ¶ Defense: "Direct public employment by a state actor is not a ‘common calling’ protected by Article IV" Defendants’ motion analyzes this case as though Plaintiff had asserted a Title VII–style claim to public employment or a constitutional right to be hired by a state actor. The Complaint makes no such claim. Plaintiff does not allege a right to employment, nor does he challenge Defendants’ authority to require on-campus work for positions so designated. The position at issue was expressly advertised as “remote-eligible.” Defendants were willing to consider and hire candidates who could work on campus, regardless of state residence. Plaintiff was denied only the opportunity to be considered for remote work based solely on his residence in Alabama, pursuant to a categorical policy excluding residents of certain states from remote eligibility. Plaintiff alleges that this residency-based exclusion from consideration for remote work burdens the right to pursue a common calling on equal terms, in violation of the Privileges and Immunities Clause of Article IV. Precedent ¶ Piper ¶ Piper specifically rejected as a 'substantial burden': administrative convenience, speculative enforcement difficulties, generalized concerns about oversight, blanked exclusions justified by "we've always done it this way." Categorical exclusions are suspect, and the state (or state actor) must justify why residents of certain states, as a class, pose a distinct problem. Defendants assert that their policy “meets the requisite standard,” but that conclusion assumes the answer to the very question presented. Under Camden and Piper, once a plaintiff plausibly alleges a residency-based burden on a common calling, the burden shifts to the state to demonstrate a substantial reason for the discrimination and a close fit between means and ends. Defendants’ motion identifies no such justification on the face of the pleadings. Camden ¶ Opinion 12(b)(2): Personal Jurisdiction ¶ Defendants did not merely make information available on the internet; they invited and processed applications from Alabama residents through a structured, interactive system, and then applied a residency-based policy to those applicants. Defendants characterize their contacts with Alabama as merely passive internet activity. But the position at issue was advertised through an interactive online system that invited applicants to create accounts, submit applications, and engage in individualized review. Defendants knowingly accepted applications from Alabama residents and then applied a residency-based exclusion to those applicants. Such conduct is not “passive” under the Zippo framework and reflects purposeful engagement with the forum. Defendants did not merely make information available on the internet; they invited and processed applications from Alabama residents through a structured, interactive system, and then applied a residency-based policy to those applicants. Plaintiff does not rely on his residence or the location of injury alone, but on Defendants’ deliberate engagement with Alabama residents through an interactive application process and application of a residency-based exclusion giving rise to this claim. Defendants knowingly accepted applications from Alabama residents and applied a residency-based exclusion to those residents through their hiring system. Defendants argue that their conduct was not directed toward Alabama. But the policy at issue operates by differentiating among states, approving residents of some states for remote eligibility while categorically excluding others. In that sense, the policy is necessarily directed at states qua states, including Alabama.

docs.text: Github Accounts ¶ A non-exhaustive list of github users to follow 🧱 Static sites, Jekyll, “boring but elegant” web ¶ GitHub Pages–adjacent, clean, sane people @github/pages-gem The canonical reference for what GitHub Pages actually supports. If something breaks locally vs Pages, this repo usually explains why. @jekyll Not flashy, but good signal on where Jekyll is going (and not going). @mmistakes (Michael Rose) Author of Minimal Mistakes theme, but more importantly: excellent examples of disciplined Jekyll structure and documentation. @simonw (Simon Willison) Static sites, personal publishing, data → HTML pipelines, zero BS. Very much your “small tools, sharp edges” vibe. 📝 Documentation, writing, developer ergonomics ¶ People who treat docs as first-class @danielecook Thoughtful writing about docs, tooling, and long-lived systems. @docwhat Docs, accessibility, clarity, and technical writing culture. @wilfred Known for diff tooling and thoughtful posts on developer experience. @karlhorky Rails-y background, but very strong opinions about maintainable docs and tooling. 🐍 Python (practical, not hype) ¶ @kennethreitz Requests, Pipenv, tooling that favors humans. @tiangolo FastAPI, excellent README hygiene, great examples of API documentation done right. @nvbn Small sharp Python tools, often very readable. 🦀 Rust (approachable, not macho) ¶ Since you’ve been flirting with Rust but not in a “rewrite the world” way: @burntsushi (Andrew Gallant) Rust done responsibly. Deep explanations, zero hype, lots of text processing. @dtolnay Procedural macros, Serde internals — heavy stuff, but impeccably documented. @matklad Rust analyzer, thoughtful design writing, very “engineer’s engineer.” 🧰 Unix, DevOps, infra minimalism ¶ People who hate unnecessary complexity: @jpetazzo Containers, infra history, thoughtful critiques of trends. @iximiuz Excellent explainer repos on Linux, containers, networking. @leandromoreira Computer architecture explainers that are actually readable.

docs.text: The Midwest ¶ Default Culture, Not Negotiated Culture Midwestern culture operates as a default system. Shared norms are assumed rather than negotiated. People are socialized to believe most others share the same expectations, so friction is managed preemptively instead of explicitly. By contrast, Southern, Appalachian, or racially mixed regions require constant cultural negotiation. There, ambiguity is owned by the speaker. In the Midwest, ambiguity is collaboratively smoothed over. This explains: • verbalized decision trees (“Light or Heavy?”), • redundancy framed as kindness, • and the absence of overt boundary-testing. ⸻ Politeness as Friction Management, Not Relational Repair Midwestern “nice” is procedural, not relational. • Interactions end once discomfort is neutralized, not once mutual recognition is confirmed. • Minimal acknowledgments (“ope”) complete the repair without waiting for response or absolution. • There is no pause for dignity exchange; the goal is to move on cleanly. This contrasts with Southern politeness, which is high-context and relational, where pauses, acknowledgment, and mutual recognition matter. ⸻ Prosody and Affect Are Intentionally Flattened Midwestern speech de-emphasizes stress, elongates non-salient vowels, and resolves downward. Prosody is used to lower emotional temperature, not to signal meaning or hierarchy. For someone from a river-based or high-context culture, this feels rhythmically wrong—like clapping off-beat—because emphasis does not align with semantic importance. ⸻ Curiosity Is Deferred, Not Absent People avoid asking personal questions until invited. Non-intrusion is a form of respect. Rapport is not built by asking about the self, but by: • narrating place, • explaining systems, • offering local knowledge. Personal curiosity unlocks only after the other person volunteers information first. ⸻ Trust Is Built Through Systems, Not Selves Midwestern trust formation centers on competence and stewardship, not disclosure. Accepted conversation anchors include: • infrastructure, • agriculture, • logistics, • geology, • co-ops, • and how things are prevented from failing. Questions about aquifers, crop rotation, or grain silos signal seriousness and respect far more than personal questions. ⸻ Aquifer Culture, Not River Culture The Midwest interior is shaped by invisible water. • Surface rivers define borders and export routes, not daily life. • Water security comes from deep aquifers, not visible flow. • Risk is delayed, invisible, and system-managed. This produces a worldview that prizes: • long-term planning, • stability, • boring competence, • and avoidance of sudden pressure changes. River cultures create narrative, hierarchy, and relational flow. Aquifer cultures create systems. ⸻ Institutions Anchor Life Quietly Churches function as civic infrastructure rather than moral theaters. • High church density exists without hellfire or emotional drama. • Church keeps time, continuity, and care networks intact. • Emotional intensity is displaced into sports, which function as the safe outlet for passion and conflict. ⸻ Belonging Is Provisional and Unceremonious There are no initiation rituals, no open suspicion, and no moment where one is declared “in.” Instead: • people are treated as equivalent by default, • trust is neither granted nor withheld explicitly, • belonging accrues through repetition, reliability, and time. This is unsettling to someone used to negotiated boundaries but is not hostile—just non-narrated.

docs.text: Drupal Install ¶ Prerequisites ¶ Debian ¶ Install the prerequisites. Most of these should already be installed. The command will only add the missing components not duplicate anything already existing. sudo apt update sudo apt install -y php-fpm php-cli php-xml php-mbstring php-gd php-curl php-zip php-mysql php-intl php-bcmath unzip git mariadb-server Check PHP version and that FPM is running. php -v systemctl status php*-fpm --no-pager Install Composer ¶ This will install Composer system-wide. cd /tmp php -r "copy('https://getcomposer.org/installer', 'composer-setup.php');" php composer-setup.php All settings correct for using Composer Downloading... Composer (version 2.9.3) successfully installed to: /tmp/composer.phar Use it: php composer.phar sudo mv composer.phar /usr/local/bin/composer composer --version Composer version 2.9.3 2025-12-30 13:40:17 PHP version 8.4.11 (/usr/bin/php8.4) Run the "diagnose" command to get more detailed diagnostics output. Set Up Drupal Environment ¶ Create Drupal Directory ¶ Create the directory mkdir /var/www/abbynet/drupal cd /var/www/abbynet/drupal Create the project cd /var/www/abbynet/drupal composer create-project drupal/recommended-project:^10 . Directory Permissions ¶ Drupal will want this directory to exist before install sudo mkdir -p /var/www/abbynet/drupal/web/sites/default/files sudo chown -R www-data:www-data /var/www/abbynet/drupal/web/sites/default/files Create Database ¶ CREATE DATABASE drupal CHARACTER SET utf8mb4 COLLATE utf8mb4_general_ci; CREATE USER 'drupaluser'@'localhost' IDENTIFIED BY 'use-a-long-password-here'; GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON drupal.* TO 'drupaluser'@'localhost'; FLUSH PRIVILEGES; EXIT; Caddy Site Block ¶ drupal.cloverdalelane.com { root * /var/www/abbynet/drupal/web basic_auth { abby <password hash> } php_fastcgi unix//run/php/php8.4-fpm.sock file_server } To find the php socket version: ls -l /run/php/ Reload Caddy ¶ sudo caddy validate --config /etc/caddy/Caddyfile sudo systemctl reload caddy Install Drupal ¶ Navigate to: https://drupal.example.com It should show the Drupal installer. Use the DB settings from earlier: Database: drupal Username: drupaluser Password: the one set Host: localhost Sanity Checks ¶ See if PHP is being executed: curl -I https://drupal.cloverdalelane.com Check Caddy logs: journalctl -u caddy -n 100 --no-pager Check php-fpm logs: journalctl -u php8.2-fpm -n 100 --no-pager

These treatments and medical procedures included, but were not limited to, [provide detailed description of physical, mental, or psychological treatments and procedures, including drugs, hormones, surgery, etc.]. docs.text: New York City, Police, Transit, and Thoughts ¶ Dear Mayor Mamdani, I am writing to propose a straightforward fiscal and governance reform regarding subway fare enforcement that would save New York City money, reduce unnecessary policing, and better align responsibility with authority. At present, fare evasion is a revenue problem borne by the Metropolitan Transportation Authority, a New York State public authority. However, the primary cost of enforcement is borne by the New York City Police Department, a city agency funded by the City of New York. This structural misalignment produces a perverse and well-documented outcome: the City spends more policing fare evasion than the MTA loses from fare evasion itself. This is not a matter of ideology or public safety “vibes.” It is a budgeting failure. The entity that controls fares and benefits from enforcement does not bear the cost of enforcing them. As a result, enforcement is overused even when it fails a basic cost-benefit test. One possible avenue is state reimbursement for subway fare enforcement. The most immediate and realistic fix is for New York State to reimburse New York City for NYPD resources devoted to fare enforcement in the subway system. This could include overtime, dedicated patrols, administrative processing, and related court costs. Once enforcement costs are internalized by the MTA or the State, policy decisions would quickly become more rational. If enforcement truly pays for itself, reimbursement will confirm that. If it does not, the current strategy would be reevaluated on its actual merits rather than political optics. Either outcome improves fiscal discipline and transparency. Barring that, honestly, the preferable option is to transfer the MTA to city control. If the State is unwilling to reimburse the City for enforcing a state agency’s revenue policy, the logical alternative is to realign authority entirely by placing the MTA under city control. This would ensure that fare policy, enforcement decisions, and budgetary consequences sit within the same governance structure. I recognize that this option is politically complex and unlikely in the near term. I raise it not as a demand, but as a reminder of the underlying principle: policy authority and financial responsibility must be aligned, or inefficiency is guaranteed. New York City should not be subsidizing state-level political priorities through its police budget. Asking the State to pay for the enforcement it demands is not radical. It is sound public finance. Thank you for your attention, and for your leadership on issues where fiscal reality and human dignity intersect. Respectfully, Abby Jane

docs.text: On Identity ¶ Being a woman means having the capacity to endure asymmetry without complaint. Before we wrap up, I want to share one personal but relevant detail. I go by Abby Jane. I am transgender, and while some of my materials still reflect my legal name, this is the name I use in the workplace and the one I would expect to use here. “Before we conclude, there’s one piece of clarity I want to offer so there are no surprises later. Professionally, I go by Abby Jane. I’m transgender, and while some materials still reflect my legal name, this is the identity I would be bringing to the role. I value transparency, and I want to make sure we’re aligned now rather than discovering misalignment down the line.”

docs.text: Queer Soup Monthly ¶ Idea ¶ On a specific day of the month (the second Wednesday; the 17th, etc.) people are invited to bring a soup/chili stew to share in the spirit of queer comraderie. People are encouraged to show up even if they haven't brought anything, but an offer to help clean or set up is appreciated. You don't have to be queer to participate, but this would be a queer space, so attendees are expected to comport themselves accordingly. Plan ¶ Use the Moon Room at Druid City Brewery. People will sign up to bring soups/stews/chilis Others will sign up to bring utensils, napkins, crackers, etc. Some may sign up to help set up or clean up The evening should be chill. Just eating and talking. I don't think there would be need for a band or entertainment (maybe get the bar to play something appropriate or people can bring vinyl?). Promotion ¶ Flyers around the bar and other places where interested people might congregate (The Alcove, Icon, the University Campus). Website for signups Social media posts Word of mouth Website ¶ Spec out a Google Forms invitation that has the correct logic Build framework around it Try to find a self-hosted system or one that is GitHub pages friendly Fundraising ¶ People can donate to help fund the soup get-together, and/or A local support group for LGBTQ+ kids in need

docs.text: Schedule ¶ 5 Days | Tokyo → Kyoto → Hiroshima → Hakone → Tokyo Birthday = Day 7 Rhythm key: - 🌸 Soft = decompression, wandering, cafés - 🌿 Medium = structured but gentle - 🔥 Intense = emotionally or physically heavy anchor DAY 1 — Arrive Tokyo ¶ 🌸 Soft | Decompression Day Arrive Narita or Haneda Train into city, hotel check- in Shower, change 20–40 min neighborhood walk Convenience- store dinner (onigiri, miso, tea) Early night Goal: land safely in your body DAY 2 — Asakusa + Ueno ¶ 🌿 Medium Senso-ji Temple Nakamise shopping street Ueno Park Tokyo National Museum (optional) Coffee + cake stop Dinner near hotel DAY 3 — Shibuya + Harajuku + Meiji Shrine ¶ 🔥 Intense (Urban Sensory Day) Meiji Jingu forest walk Harajuku wandering Shibuya Crossing (once, reverently) Loft or Tokyu Hands 1 Daikanyama dinner DAY 4 — Museums + Bookstores ¶ 🌸 Soft Mori Art Museum Jimbocho used bookstores Stationery stores Café hopping Long bath + skincare night DAY 5 — Neighborhood Day ( Shimokitazawa or Koenji ) ¶ 🌿 Medium Vintage shops Indie bookstores Vinyl shops People-watching cafés Dinner at a tiny local place DAY 6 — Flex / Rest / Rain Buffer ¶ 🌸 Soft Anything you skipped Light shopping Hair or spa Or literally nothing DAY 7 — 🎂 Birthday Day in Tokyo ¶ 🔥 Intense (But Romantic, Not Heavy) (This is your sacred pivot day.) Morning shrine visit (gratitude ritual) Solo gift purchase for yourself Fancy lunch Nezu Museum Sunset river walk High- end dessert café Champagne in your hotel room DAY 8 — Tokyo → Kyoto ¶ 🌿 Medium (Transit + Arrival) Bullet train to Kyoto Hotel check- in Kamo River walk Dinner in Pontocho Early night DAY 9 — Eastern Kyoto Temples ¶ 🔥 Intense (Spiritual + Physical) Kiyomizu-dera Sannenzaka / Ninenzaka Yasaka Shrine Tea ceremony Long slow dinner DAY 10 — Philosopher’s Path + Ginkaku- ji ¶ 🌸 Soft Canal walk Silver Pavilion Bookshop café Journaling afternoon Light dinner DAY 11 — Arashiyama ¶ 🌿 Medium Bamboo grove (early) Tenryu- ji River walk Monkey park (optional) Afternoon nap DAY 12 — Kyoto → Hiroshima ¶ 🌿 Medium (Transit + Emotional Setup) Bullet train to Hiroshima Hotel check- in Evening river walk Quiet dinner Early night DAY 13 — Hiroshima Peace Day ¶ 🔥 Intense (Moral Anchor Day) Peace Memorial Park Atomic Bomb Dome Hiroshima Peace Museum Long silent river walk Simple dinner Early bed (You will be emotionally done. This is correct.) DAY 14 — Hiroshima → Hakone ¶ 🌿 Medium (Transit + Reset) Morning train to Hakone Ryokan check- in Private onsen Kaiseki dinner Early night DAY 15 — Hakone → Tokyo → Fly Home ¶ 🌸 Soft (Closure Day) Early onsen soak Train to Tokyo Airport train Reflection Cry softly on the plane Two department stores ↩

docs.text: De-Customization ¶ Purpose ¶ This framework is intended as a practical tool for registrar leadership teams undertaking SIS modernization, ERP upgrades, or moving to cloud and/or SaaS environments. It offers a structured way to inventory, evaluate, and intentionally retain or retire customizations without flattening legitimate academic complexity. The goal is not to reduce customization for its own sake, but to make institutional complexity explicit, governable, and sustainable over time. Guiding Principle ¶ What is "de-customization": Systematically reducing institution-specific logic that increases technical debt, impedes upgrades, and embeds obsolete policy assumptions, while preserving the academic values that motivated the customizations in the first place. Framework ¶ Questions to guide the process: What customizations do we have? What are the parameters of the customization? What limitations of the baseline SIS made the customization necessary? Is the process for which the customization was built still used; has it materially changed since implementation? For each customization, is it still necessary, is the process simply unable to be incorporated into baseline, if not, what changes can be reasonably made to the process so it can be integrated into baseline? 1. Inventory and Visibility ¶ Identify all non-baseline elements , including: Database-level modifications Custom scripts / middleware Configuration overrides that behave like code Manual workarounds that function as “shadow customizations” Document : Owner Date implemented Triggering requirement (policy, accreditation, faculty demand, historical artifact) 2. Define the Scope and Nature of Each Customization ¶ Classify each customization as one of: Policy-driven (e.g., grading exceptions, registration priority) Pedagogical (tutorial systems, narrative evaluation workflows) Compliance-driven (VA, financial aid, visa tracking) Historical workaround (baseline once lacked a feature) Cultural preference (“we’ve always done it this way”) This matters because only some categories deserve preservation pressure . 3. Interrogate the Original Constraint ¶ Was baseline truly incapable, or merely unfamiliar? Has the vendor since: Added baseline support? Shifted philosophy (e.g., from transactional to workflow-driven)? Was the customization compensating for: Weak governance Policy ambiguity Faculty needs that have since changed Has the system changed, but the customization hasn't? 4. Assess Process Drift ¶ For each customization, is the underlying academic or administrative process: Still in use? Materially changed? Under active debate? Has the customization: Frozen a process that should have evolved? Become de facto policy? 5. Decision Matrix: Retire, Refactor, Retain ¶ For each customization: Retire Criteria If baseline now supports it If the process no longer exists Requires Policy owner sign-off Data migration or archival plan Communication to affected stakeholders Refactor into configuration Criteria If logic can be expressed via delivered tools Requires Vendor validation Test environment modeling Rollback plan Documentation update Training for users Retain (explicity and defensively) Criteria If it reflects a core academic value If eliminating it would materially harm pedagogy Requires Named business owner Justification tied to institutional mission Upgrade/migration impact assessment Timeline for periodic review The guiding principle is that retention becomes an intentional act , not simply inertia. Keys ¶ Faculty, student, and staff needs are real and important. Academic exceptionalism is a feature. The staff/faculty own the logic. No one is being replaced. Technical debt is necessary only when it serves a purpose that coincides with the academic, cultural, and social mission of the College. Systems encode values whether we intend them to or not. Quotes ¶ Many institutions were afraid to touch student systems, especially customizations, because no one remembered why they existed. That’s where the real risk lives. The goal isn’t to remove what works, but to understand it well enough that we can support it sustainably, upgrade safely, and explain it clearly to the next group of people who inherit it. The goal not here to simplify the College. Rather, it is to make its complexity intentional and sustainable. The first thing to understand is: what in the system matters most to the College, and what would feel like a loss if we got it wrong? Abby Jane Morton Prepared as part of a University Registrar search process January 2026

docs.text: Definition ¶ An organization that operates in a high-risk, complex, tightly coupled environment and nevertheless achieves an exceptionally low rate of catastrophic failure over long periods of time, not because it eliminates error, but because it continuously detects, contains, and learns from it. Why This Matters for Registrars ¶ Registrar operations share the same risk profile as classic high-reliability environments: low tolerance for error, tightly coupled processes, irreversible downstream consequences, and deadline-driven surges. Treating the registrar’s office explicitly as a high-reliability organization reframes everyday academic administration as safety-critical work rather than clerical throughput. Qualities ¶ What it is. ¶ Preoccupation with failure Errors, near-misses, anomalies are signals Success today doesn't imply safety tomorrow Actively looks for what almost goes wrong This is the opposite of "no news is good news." Reluctance to simplify interpretations Avoids single-cause explanations Encourages multiple perspectives Resists "this is an unimportant process" This is directly counter to the "operator error" reflex. Sensitivity to operations Maintains situational awareness of front-line processes Values operational/experiential insight over abstract metrics Knows what the work looks like not just what the procedures dictate This is where most bureaucracies fail. Commitment to resilience Assumes things will go wrong Trains for both recovery and prevention Practices graceful degradation rather than brittle perfection This is key to "normal accident" prevention. Deference to expertise Authority rests with the person who has the most relevant knowledge not position Rank yields to situational competence Escalation is normalized, encouraged, and, most importantly, not punished The appropriate person makes and documents the decision. What it is not ¶ Cultures that expect zero defects Obtuse or jargon heavy documentation Rigidly hierarchical Compliance-heavy bureaucracies Check-list obsessed Punishment-driven The Registrar's Office ¶ The Registrar's office's role ¶ A registrar’s office is responsible for records whose correctness must be near-perfect 1 , at scale 2 , under constant change 3 , with non-negotiable deadlines 4 and asymmetric consequences 5 . 1. An institution is only as credible as its records. 2. Records arrive in volume. 3. Institutions change unexpectedly. 4. Enforcement of deadlines is a core mission of the office. 5. Small errors can have huge downstream harm. High Reliability Registrar's Office ¶ Preoccupation with failure Assume the system will break somewhere. In practice: Near misses are signals even if the end-result was successful Actively tracks errors and error origination Design processes that expect mistakes or malfunctions of systems Dual controls for high-impact processes (degree posting, academic standing) Procedural implementation: Validation rules that block action not just warn Routine auditing of data and processes Staff implications Staff are actively encouraged to surface problems without fearing blame "I think I made a mistake" is expected and rewarded rather than punished Reluctance to simplify interpretations Resist the urge to flatten complexity. In practice: Edge cases are not considered "close enough" Document a process's exceptions and quirks Know that policy can be technically simple but operationally complex Procedural implementation: Clear exception pathways (no shadow processes or tribal knowledge) Documentation that explains both why and how Systems designed to reflect nuance instead of implementing workarounds Staff implications Staff are empowered actors not button-pushers Expertise and correctness is valued over speed Senior staff are consulted not because of rank, but because of accumulated pattern recognition Sensitivity to operations Stay close to the work as it actually happens. In practice: Leadership understands and plans for peak stress points Decisions are based on peak stress points not quiet times Procedural implementation Visibility of queues and bottlenecks Clearly delineated roles within the office and between other offices Term calendars treated as an operational risk map, not just procedural dates Staff implications Managers observe workflows (both physical and electronic) Staff are part of the process change system "This will break" is taken seriously Commitment to resilience We don’t just prevent failure; we recover gracefully. In practice: Recognize the office already does crisis response High reliability means formalization of that response Procedural implementation Documentation includes failure and recovery playbooks; what to do in case of catastrophe Cross-training is routine and formalized Versioning of documentation; what changed when did it change Staff implications Staff are empowered and trained to diagnose and respond Institutional memory is written not kept as individual knowledge Practiced recovery is planned for and the need for heroics are minimized Deference to expertise Authority rests with whomever knows the most right now. In practice: The person who understands a process leads during peak times All staff are involved in integration and testing The person who understands a process handles edge cases For the preceding, rank and title are irrelevant and ignored Procedural implementation Clear escalation paths based on expertise not rank Decisions are documented and include why a decision was made Elimination on single points of failure Staff implications Roles are defined by responsibility and expertise Career paths emphasize systems thinking and policy mastery Anyone and everyone is empowered to yell STOP What does this mean? ¶ Staff are: data stewards policy translators quality assurance professionals risk managers Automation: doesn't devalue people; it elevates them staff focus is on interpretation, exception handling, and system oversight Success is measure by error prevention and recovery; not processing volume The Conclusion A registrar’s office is already a high-reliability organization; the work only becomes sustainable when we design processes, roles, organizational culture, and governance structures that treat accuracy, resilience, and expertise as the core mission, not side effects. Abby Jane Morton Prepared as part of a University Registrar search process January 2026

docs.title: What does this mean?

docs.text: Overriding Beliefs ¶ Student-first means removing friction from the people who serve students directly. Technology is a means not an end unto itself. A goal of mine is that no one using the systems I oversee knows I exist. My responsibility isn't only to the student in front of me; but all future, current, and former students; every student deserves credentials that are credible
